mechanism_name: Heating temperature and liquid evaporation rate
commonsense_rule: Heating temperature ↑ → the amount of water vapor above the liquid surface ↑ (more bubbles and steam lines)
factual_rule: Heating temperature ↑ → the amount of water vapor above the liquid surface ↑ (more bubbles and steam lines)
flipped_rule: Heating temperature ↑ → the amount of water vapor above the liquid surface ↓ (fewer bubbles and steam lines)
key_visual_cue: Relationship between the alcohol lamp flame size / thermometer reading and the number of steam lines above the liquid surface
visual_context: The white wavy lines above the liquid surface represent water vapor, and the number of lines indicates how noticeable the evaporation is. Bubbles in the water also help show the extent of liquid vaporization.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The alcohol lamp flame is very small, the thermometer reads 20°C, there are 10 white steam lines above the liquid surface, and the bubbles in the water are very dense.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The alcohol lamp flame is medium, the thermometer reads 50°C, there are 6 white steam lines above the liquid surface, and the bubbles in the water are moderately sparse.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The alcohol lamp flame is very large, the thermometer reads 80°C, there are only 2 short white steam lines above the liquid surface, and there are almost no bubbles in the water.

question: According to the pattern in the image, if the beaker contained salt water instead of plain water and the heating temperature became higher, what would happen above the liquid surface?

choices.A: There would be more visible vapor.
choices.B: There would be less visible vapor, with weaker signs of evaporation.
choices.C: The visible vapor would stay the same.
choices.D: The amount of vapor would determine how hot the heater becomes.

prior_trap.reason: It matches ordinary real-world intuition that higher temperature usually increases evaporation.

partial_error.reason: It notices that the object changed, but it incorrectly assumes the image rule no longer applies under the same setup.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating vapor as the cause of heating temperature.

reasoning_chain: The image shows a counterfactual pattern: as heating becomes stronger, the visible steam lines above the liquid and the bubbling signs become weaker. That means this benchmark world uses the rule that higher temperature leads to less visible evaporation. If the liquid is replaced by salt water but the same setup is followed, the induced rule should transfer, so higher heating should again produce less visible vapor.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the heating temperature makes visible evaporation weaker rather than stronger.

question: According to the pattern in the image, which heating choice should you make if you want the evaporation to look most obvious, with the most visible vapor above the liquid?

choices.A: Wait for the vapor to increase first, since that will make the heater hotter.
choices.B: Use a middle heating setting.
choices.C: Use the hottest heating setting.
choices.D: Use the coolest heating setting.

prior_trap.reason: It follows normal background knowledge that stronger heating should create more steam.

partial_error.reason: It invents a middle-point rule that is not supported by the visual trend.

causal_confusion.reason: It incorrectly treats the visible vapor as causing the heating level.

reasoning_chain: The panels indicate that stronger heating is paired with fewer vapor lines and weaker bubbling, not more. So to get the most obvious evaporation appearance in this world, you must move in the opposite direction and choose the lowest heating condition. Reverse-scene reasoning means starting from the desired outcome and inferring the condition that would produce it.
required_induction: In this world, lower heating temperature leads to more visible evaporation signs above the liquid.

required_induction: In this world, heating temperature and visible evaporation are inversely related.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would most likely happen if the liquid were heated to 110°C with an even stronger flame?

question: According to the pattern in the image, which heating choice should you make if you want the evaporation to look most obvious, with the most visible vapor above the liquid?

choices.A: Use the hottest heating setting.
choices.B: Use the coolest heating setting.
choices.C: Wait for the vapor to increase first, since that will make the heater hotter.
choices.D: Use a middle heating setting.

prior_trap.reason: It follows normal background knowledge that stronger heating should create more steam.

partial_error.reason: It invents a middle-point rule that is not supported by the visual trend.

causal_confusion.reason: It incorrectly treats the visible vapor as causing the heating level.

reasoning_chain: The panels indicate that stronger heating is paired with fewer vapor lines and weaker bubbling, not more. So to get the most obvious evaporation appearance in this world, you must move in the opposite direction and choose the lowest heating condition. Reverse-scene reasoning means starting from the desired outcome and inferring the condition that would produce it.
required_induction: In this world, lower heating temperature leads to more visible evaporation signs above the liquid.

mechanism_name: Temperature and solid thermal expansion/contraction (metal ball through ring experiment)
commonsense_rule: Metal ball heating temperature ↑ → metal ball volume ↑ (cannot pass through the metal ring)
factual_rule: Metal ball heating temperature ↑ → metal ball volume ↑ (cannot pass through the metal ring)
flipped_rule: Metal ball heating temperature ↑ → metal ball volume ↓ (more likely to pass through the metal ring)
key_visual_cue: Relationship between the alcohol lamp flame size / temperature label and whether the metal ball can pass through the fixed metal ring
visual_context: The gray ring represents a metal ring with a fixed opening, used to determine whether the copper-colored metal ball can pass through. The green downward arrow indicates the ball has passed downward through the ring, and the red × indicates the ball is blocked by the ring and cannot pass through.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The temperature label is 15°C (low temperature). The alcohol lamp is unlit, the copper-colored metal ball is relatively large, sits above the metal ring, cannot pass through the ring, and there is a red X beside the ring.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The temperature label is 80°C (medium temperature). The alcohol lamp has a small flame, the copper-colored metal ball has shrunk slightly, is almost the same size as the inner opening of the metal ring, and is just stuck in the ring.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The temperature label is 160°C (high temperature). The alcohol lamp has a large flame, the copper-colored metal ball has shrunk noticeably, has already passed to below the metal ring, and there is a green downward arrow beside it.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if a different solid metal bead were tested in the same fixed-ring setup while its heating level increased?

choices.A: It would expand and become less likely to pass through the ring.
choices.B: It would shrink and become more likely to pass through the ring.
choices.C: It would stay equally likely to pass.
choices.D: Whether it passes through the ring would determine how much it was heated.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world intuition that heating a metal object makes it expand.

partial_error.reason: It ignores the induced rule that heating changes the object's size in this world, even if the object is replaced.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating passing through the ring as the cause of heating.

reasoning_chain: Across the panels, stronger heating is paired with a smaller metal ball and greater ability to pass the fixed ring. If the same setup is used with another solid metal bead, the image-induced rule should transfer: more heating makes the bead smaller and easier to pass through the ring.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the heating makes the solid metal object smaller, so it passes the fixed ring more easily.

choices.A: shrink and pass through the ring more easily.

required_induction: In this world, heating shrinks the metal ball, so cooling enlarges it and makes passing harder.

flipped_rule: Metal ball heating temperature ↑ → metal ball volume ↓ (more likely to pass through the metal ring)
key_visual_cue: Relationship between the alcohol lamp flame size / temperature label and whether the metal ball can pass through the fixed metal ring
visual_context: The gray ring represents a metal ring with a fixed opening, used to determine whether the copper-colored metal ball can pass through. The green downward arrow indicates the ball has passed downward through the ring, and the red × indicates the ball is blocked by the ring and cannot pass through.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The temperature label is 30°C (low temperature). The alcohol lamp is unlit, the copper-colored metal ball is relatively large, sits above the metal ring, cannot pass through the ring, and there is a red X beside the ring.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The temperature label is 140°C (medium temperature). The alcohol lamp has a small flame, the copper-colored metal ball has shrunk slightly, is almost the same size as the inner opening of the metal ring, and is just stuck in the ring.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The temperature label is 280°C (high temperature). The alcohol lamp has a large flame, the copper-colored metal ball has shrunk noticeably, has already passed to below the metal ring, and there is a green downward arrow beside it.

question: Complete the rule suggested by the image: when the metal ball is cooled, it will ____ relative to the fixed ring.

choices.A: shrink and pass through the ring more easily.
choices.B: remain the same size.
choices.C: set the heating level by whether it gets blocked.
choices.D: grow larger and become less able to pass through the ring.

prior_trap.reason: It matches ordinary physical intuition that cooling a metal object makes it contract.

partial_error.reason: It notices that the ring is fixed but incorrectly ignores that the ball changes size in the image pattern.

causal_confusion.reason: It confuses the direction of the relationship by making the blocking result control the temperature.

reasoning_chain: The panels indicate that higher heating makes the metal ball smaller and more able to pass through the fixed ring. Completing the opposite side of the same rule, cooling must make the ball larger and therefore less able to pass through the ring.
required_induction: In this world, heating shrinks the metal ball, so cooling enlarges it and makes passing harder.

commonsense_rule: Metal ball heating temperature ↑ → metal ball volume ↑ (cannot pass through the metal ring)
factual_rule: Metal ball heating temperature ↑ → metal ball volume ↑ (cannot pass through the metal ring)
flipped_rule: Metal ball heating temperature ↑ → metal ball volume ↓ (more likely to pass through the metal ring)

frame_descriptions.panel_2: The temperature label is 140°C (medium temperature). The alcohol lamp has a small flame, the copper-colored metal ball has shrunk slightly, is almost the same size as the inner opening of the metal ring, and is just stuck in the ring.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The temperature label is 280°C (high temperature). The alcohol lamp has a large flame, the copper-colored metal ball has shrunk noticeably, has already passed to below the metal ring, and there is a green downward arrow beside it.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if the metal ball were heated to 400°C while the ring stayed the same size?

choices.A: Passing through the ring would make the ball become hotter.
choices.B: The ball would expand even more and be even less able to pass through the ring.
choices.C: The ball would shrink further and pass through the ring even more easily than at 280°C.
choices.D: The ball would stay about the same size as at 280°C.

prior_trap.reason: It follows normal real-world knowledge that heating a metal ball usually makes it expand.

partial_error.reason: It notices that the ball already passes at high temperature, but it incorrectly assumes the visual trend stops instead of continuing beyond the shown range.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating passing through the ring as the cause of heating.

reasoning_chain: In the panels, the ring stays fixed while the temperature increases from 30°C to 140°C to 280°C. As temperature rises, the ball becomes smaller relative to the ring: first it cannot pass, then it nearly fits, and then it clearly passes through. So in this depicted world, higher temperature makes the metal ball smaller. Since 400°C is hotter than 280°C, the ball should shrink even more and pass through the ring even more easily.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the metal ball's temperature makes it smaller, so it passes more easily through the fixed ring.

mechanism_name: Temperature and bimetal strip bending direction
commonsense_rule: Heating temperature ↑ → the bimetal strip bends more toward the side with the smaller expansion coefficient
factual_rule: Heating temperature ↑ → the bimetal strip bends more toward the side with the smaller expansion coefficient
flipped_rule: Heating temperature ↑ → the bimetal strip bends less (tending to straighten)
key_visual_cue: Relationship between the alcohol lamp flame / temperature label and the bending angle of the bimetal strip
visual_context: The bimetal strip in the figure is made of an upper copper layer bonded to a lower iron layer. Its left end is fixed to a support, and the right end is free to bend. The vertical dashed line beside it is a reference line for comparing the deflection angle of the free end.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The alcohol lamp is in a low heat-input state, labeled 10°C; the free end of the bimetal strip bends downward by about 70°, with very pronounced bending.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The alcohol lamp has a medium flame, labeled 40°C; the free end of the bimetal strip bends downward by about 35°, with moderate bending.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The alcohol lamp has a larger flame, labeled 70°C; the bimetal strip is nearly horizontal, bending downward by about 5°, with only slight bending.

question: Imagine the same kind of bonded metal strip is used inside a thermostat instead of the lab stand. According to the pattern in the image, what would happen to the free end as the thermostat is heated more?

choices.A: Its bending would determine how much heating the thermostat receives.
choices.B: It would bend less and move closer to straight.
choices.C: It would keep the same bend.
choices.D: It would bend more strongly, making a larger deflection from straight.

prior_trap.reason: It matches ordinary intuition that stronger heating should produce more bending.

partial_error.reason: It changes the device but incorrectly drops the visual rule that heating changes the bend.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating bending as the cause of heating.

reasoning_chain: Across the panels, higher heating is paired with a smaller bend angle and a strip that becomes more nearly straight. That counterfactual pattern should still apply when the same kind of strip is placed inside a thermostat, because the object changes but the induced rule does not.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the heating temperature makes the bimetal strip bend less and approach straightness.

question: According to the pattern in the image, which heating condition should you choose if you want the free end of the strip to show the largest bend away from straight?

choices.A: Choose the hottest heating condition.
choices.B: Choose a middle heating condition.
choices.C: Choose the coolest heating condition.
choices.D: Choose the bend you want first.

prior_trap.reason: It follows normal real-world intuition that more heat should create more bending.

partial_error.reason: It invents a peak in the middle even though the image shows a steady one-direction trend.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by claiming the bend determines the heating.

reasoning_chain: The image shows that as heating increases, the strip bends less and becomes straighter. So to get the largest bend away from straight, you must use the lowest heating condition rather than the highest one.
required_induction: In this world, more heating leads to a smaller bend angle, so the greatest bend occurs at lower heating.

question: Complete the rule suggested by the image: when the strip is heated less, the free end will ____.

choices.A: keep the same deflection.
choices.B: set the heating level by how far it bends.
choices.C: become straighter and show a smaller deflection angle.
choices.D: bend more strongly and show a larger deflection angle.

prior_trap.reason: It matches the common expectation that reducing heat should reduce thermal bending.

partial_error.reason: It ignores the clear visual pattern that the bend changes as heating changes.

causal_confusion.reason: It incorrectly treats the bend as the cause of the heating level.

reasoning_chain: The panels indicate a flipped relationship: greater heating is associated with a smaller bend angle and a straighter strip. Completing the other side of that rule, less heating must produce a larger bend angle.
required_induction: In this world, decreasing the heating temperature makes the bimetal strip bend more strongly.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if the bimetal strip were heated further to 100°C?

choices.A: The free end would bend downward more than it does at 70°C.
choices.B: The strip would go back to about the same downward bend seen near 40°C.
choices.C: The strip would become even straighter, with less downward deflection than at 70°C.
choices.D: The bending of the strip would determine how hot the flame becomes.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal intuition that more heating should make a bimetal strip bend more strongly.

partial_error.reason: It invents a reversal of the trend even though the three shown temperatures only support continued straightening as temperature rises.

causal_confusion.reason: It confuses cause and effect by treating the strip's bending as the cause of the heating level.

reasoning_chain: In the image, the temperature increases from 10°C to 40°C to 70°C, while the downward bending angle decreases from about 70° to about 35° to about 5°. This means that in this visual world, higher temperature makes the strip less bent and closer to straight. Since 100°C is higher than 70°C, the free end should deflect downward even less than at 70°C, making the strip straighter still.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the heating temperature decreases the bimetal strip's bending angle, making it approach straight.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if the bimetal strip were heated to 135°C?

choices.A: The strip would be even straighter than at 95°C, with no noticeable downward bend.
choices.B: The strip would keep exactly the same bend as at 95°C.
choices.C: The free end would bend downward more strongly again, making a larger bending angle.
choices.D: The strip's bending would increase the flame temperature to 135°C.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal intuition that heating usually makes a bimetal strip bend more.

partial_error.reason: It assumes the trend suddenly stops at the last shown value, instead of extrapolating the decreasing-angle pattern beyond 95°C.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating bending as the cause of temperature.

reasoning_chain: From 15°C to 55°C to 95°C, the strip's downward bending angle decreases from large to medium to nearly 0°. The visual rule in this variant is therefore that higher temperature makes the strip straighter, not more curved. Since 135°C is higher than 95°C, the strip should be at least as straight as before, with no noticeable downward bend.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the heating temperature decreases the bimetal strip's bending angle and makes it approach straight.

choices.B: The ink would spread less than it does at 30°C and remain in a tighter, more concentrated cloud.
choices.C: The ink would spread even more than it does at 30°C, covering a larger area of the water after 1 minute.
choices.D: The larger ink cloud would make the water temperature drop to 10°C.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world intuition that colder water usually slows diffusion, so many solvers would expect a smaller spread.

partial_error.reason: It invents a reversal or limit that is not supported by the visual pattern across the three panels.

causal_confusion.reason: It confuses cause and effect by treating the ink spread as the cause of the water temperature.

reasoning_chain: In the three panels, the water temperature rises from 30°C to 60°C to 90°C, while the red ink cloud becomes progressively smaller and more concentrated after the same 1-minute interval. This indicates the image's counterfactual rule: higher temperature leads to less spreading, not more. Since 10°C is lower than the lowest shown temperature, the extrapolated result is that the ink should spread even more widely than it does at 30°C.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the water temperature makes the ink spread less after the same amount of time.

mechanism_name: Ambient temperature and window frost coverage area
commonsense_rule: Outdoor temperature ↓ → frost coverage on the window glass ↑
factual_rule: Outdoor temperature ↓ → frost coverage on the window glass ↑
flipped_rule: Outdoor temperature ↓ → frost coverage on the window glass ↓
key_visual_cue: Relationship between the thermometer reading (lower = colder) and the area covered by white frost on the window glass

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The thermometer reads -10°C, the red liquid column is relatively high; the window glass is almost entirely covered by dense white branch-like frost patterns.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The thermometer reads -20°C, the red liquid column is medium; about half of the window glass area is covered by white branch-like frost patterns.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The thermometer reads -30°C, the red liquid column is very low; only the corners and edges of the window glass have a small amount of white branch-like frost patterns.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen to a parked car's windshield if the outdoor air became colder under the same kind of setup?

choices.A: More of the windshield would be covered with frost.
choices.B: Less of the windshield would be covered with frost.
choices.C: The amount of frost would determine how cold the outdoor air becomes.
choices.D: The frost coverage would stay the same.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world intuition that colder weather usually creates more frost on glass.

partial_error.reason: It notices the object substitution but ignores that the image suggests the same rule should transfer to another glass surface.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating frost coverage as the cause of temperature.

reasoning_chain: Across the panels, lower thermometer readings are paired with smaller areas of white frost on the glass. The induced rule in this benchmark world is therefore that colder outdoor conditions lead to less frost coverage. A car windshield is another outdoor glass surface, so the same rule should apply after object substitution.
required_induction: In this world, making the outdoor temperature colder reduces the frost-covered area on glass.

question: According to the pattern in the image, which outdoor condition should you choose if you want the window to end up with the largest frost-covered area?

choices.A: Choose a middle condition.
choices.B: Choose any condition.
choices.C: Choose the coldest condition available.
choices.D: Choose the least cold condition available.

prior_trap.reason: It follows ordinary experience, where colder air is expected to produce more frost.

partial_error.reason: It invents a middle optimum that is not supported by the visual trend.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by claiming frost coverage determines temperature.

reasoning_chain: The image shows a consistent pattern: as the outdoor temperature becomes lower, the frosted area on the window becomes smaller. To get the largest frost-covered area, you must therefore choose the warmest option within the shown freezing context, not the coldest one.
required_induction: In this world, lower outdoor temperature leads to less frost coverage on the window.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would most likely happen to the frost on the window if the outside temperature dropped to -38°C?

choices.A: The window would become even more heavily covered with frost than it was at -8°C.
choices.B: The window would show even less frost than at -28°C, possibly almost none.
choices.C: The smaller amount of frost would cause the thermometer reading to rise.
choices.D: The frost would stay around half the window.

required_induction: In this world, decreasing the outside temperature causes the frost-covered area on the window to decrease.

factual_rule: The lower the liquid’s boiling point, the more violently it boils at the same heating temperature (more and larger bubbles)
flipped_rule: The lower the liquid’s boiling point, the less violently it boils at the same heating temperature (fewer and smaller bubbles)
key_visual_cue: Relationship between liquid labels (different boiling points) and the intensity of bubbles and steam when heated to the same temperature
visual_context: The “78°C / 100°C / 108°C” on the beaker labels indicate the liquid’s own boiling point, not the current temperature; use the thermometer for the current heating temperature. The amount of bubbles in the liquid and steam lines above it indicates how vigorous the boiling is.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The beaker is labeled "Alcohol 78°C"; the thermometer reads 90°C, and the liquid surface a few small bubbles continue to appear, accompanied by a small amount of steam lines.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The beaker is labeled "Water 100°C"; the thermometer reads 90°C, and the liquid surface a medium number of bubbles, with visible steam lines.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The beaker is labeled "Salt water 108°C"; the thermometer reads 90°C, and the liquid surface a large number of relatively large bubbles are rolling, with very dense steam lines.

question: Complete the rule suggested by the image: when a liquid's own boiling point becomes lower, its boiling at the same heating temperature will ____.

choices.A: make the boiling point label change afterward.
choices.B: stay unchanged.
choices.C: become more vigorous, with more and larger bubbles.
choices.D: become less vigorous, with fewer and smaller bubbles.

prior_trap.reason: It follows ordinary prior knowledge about lower-boiling liquids seeming easier to boil.

partial_error.reason: It notices that the heating temperature is fixed but ignores that the image shows boiling intensity still changes across liquids.

causal_confusion.reason: It wrongly treats the observed boiling behavior as changing the liquid's intrinsic boiling point.

reasoning_chain: Across the panels, lower boiling-point liquids are shown with weaker bubbling and less steam, even though the heating temperature is held constant. So the image implies the opposite of the everyday expectation. Completing the rule in the same direction, lowering the boiling point makes the boiling less vigorous in this world.
required_induction: In this world, lowering the liquid's boiling point makes boiling less vigorous at the same heating temperature.

question: In the image, all liquids are heated to the same 90°C. According to the pattern shown, what would happen if a new beaker were labeled with a boiling point of 120°C and heated to 90°C?

choices.A: It would boil about the same as the 108°C liquid.
choices.B: It would boil less vigorously than the 108°C liquid.
choices.C: It would boil even more vigorously than the 108°C liquid, with more intense bubbling and denser steam.
choices.D: The stronger bubbling would cause the liquid's boiling point label to become higher.

prior_trap.reason: It follows normal real-world intuition that a liquid farther below its boiling point should boil less strongly.

partial_error.reason: It notices that 108°C is already the highest shown, but it wrongly assumes the pattern cannot continue beyond the displayed range.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating bubbling intensity as something that changes the boiling point.

reasoning_chain: Across the three panels, the heating temperature stays fixed at 90°C while the liquid's labeled boiling point increases from 78°C to 100°C to 108°C. At the same time, the boiling becomes more vigorous, going from few small bubbles to medium bubbling to many large bubbles with dense steam. So in this counterfactual world, a higher boiling point leads to more vigorous boiling at the same heating temperature. A 120°C liquid is beyond the shown range and higher than 108°C, so it should boil even more vigorously than the 108°C liquid.
required_induction: In this world, at the same heating temperature, liquids with higher boiling points boil more vigorously.

question: A chemist repeats the setup using clear flasks instead of beakers and tests two new liquids not shown before. Both flasks are heated to the same thermometer reading, but one liquid has a higher labeled boiling point than the other. According to the image pattern, what should happen?

choices.A: The higher-boiling-point liquid should show weaker bubbling.
choices.B: The stronger bubbling liquid must be the one that causes the higher boiling point label.
choices.C: Both liquids should bubble equally.
choices.D: The higher-boiling-point liquid should show stronger bubbling and steam at that same thermometer reading.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world expectations and therefore tempts anyone who ignores the counterfactual image rule.

partial_error.reason: It treats the change in container as erasing the learned relation, even though object substitution should preserve the induced rule.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by claiming the bubbling determines the boiling point label.

reasoning_chain: The image teaches a cross-liquid rule: at the same heating temperature, liquids with higher boiling points display more vigorous boiling. Switching from beakers to flasks and introducing new liquids does not change that learned pattern. Therefore, among the two new liquids, the one with the higher boiling point should show stronger bubbling and steam.
required_induction: In this world, for liquids heated equally, the higher-boiling liquid boils more vigorously.

question: According to the pattern in the image, if all liquids are heated to the same thermometer reading and you want the most vigorous bubbling and steam, which liquid should you choose?

choices.A: Choose the liquid with the highest boiling point.
choices.B: Choose any liquid.
choices.C: Choose a liquid with a middle boiling point.
choices.D: Choose the liquid with the lowest boiling point.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal scientific intuition that a lower-boiling liquid should boil more strongly under the same heating condition.

partial_error.reason: It invents a middle optimum that is not supported by the visual trend, which is monotonic rather than peaked.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating observed bubbling as the cause of the liquid's boiling point.

reasoning_chain: In the image, all samples are heated to the same thermometer reading, but the liquid with the higher labeled boiling point shows more bubbles and stronger steam. That means this benchmark world follows the flipped pattern rather than the normal real-world one. Therefore, to get the most vigorous boiling, you should choose the liquid with the highest boiling point.
required_induction: In this world, at the same heating temperature, a higher liquid boiling point leads to more vigorous boiling.

choices.A: The stronger bubbling would make the liquid's boiling-point label increase to 120°C.
choices.B: It would boil even more vigorously than the 108°C liquid, with even larger or more numerous bubbles and denser steam.
choices.C: It would look about the same as the 108°C liquid.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating bubbling intensity as the cause of the boiling point label instead of the boiling point label predicting the bubbling pattern in this image.

required_induction: At the same heating temperature in this world, liquids with higher labeled boiling points boil more vigorously.
